careerLearnMore.Data Analyst: Data analysts collect, clean, and analyze data to help organizations make decisions. You might build dashboards, run reports, or find trends. Strong Excel and SQL skills are common; many roles also use Python or R. Entry-level titles include Business Analyst or Reporting Analyst.
careerLearnMore.Financial Analyst: Financial analysts evaluate investments, budgets, and financial performance. You could work in corporate finance, banking, or investment firms. The role often involves modeling, forecasting, and presenting to stakeholders. A CFA or MBA can help with advancement.
careerLearnMore.Accountant: Accountants prepare and review financial records, ensure compliance with tax and reporting rules, and advise on efficiency. You can work in public accounting (audit/tax), industry, or government. CPA licensure is required for many senior and public accounting roles.
careerLearnMore.Management Consultant: Consultants help organizations solve problems and improve performance. You might work on strategy, operations, or technology. The work is project-based and often involves research, interviews, and presenting recommendations. Travel and long hours are common; exit opportunities are strong.
careerLearnMore.Marketing Specialist: Marketing specialists develop and run campaigns across digital, social, email, or traditional channels. You might focus on content, analytics, or brand. Roles often require creativity plus comfort with data and tools like Google Analytics or social platforms.
careerLearnMore.Human Resources Specialist: HR specialists support recruiting, benefits, training, and employee relations. You might screen candidates, run onboarding, or handle policy questions. Strong communication and discretion are important. Many grow into HR business partner or specialist roles (e.g., compensation, L&D).
careerLearnMore.Project Manager: Project managers plan, track, and deliver projects on time and on budget. You coordinate people and tasks, manage risks, and report to stakeholders. Certifications like PMP or CAPM are common. Used in tech, construction, healthcare, and many other industries.
careerLearnMore.Sales Representative: Sales reps connect products or services with customers through outreach, demos, and relationship building. Roles range from inside sales (calls/emails) to field sales. Compensation often includes base plus commission. Strong communication and resilience are key.
careerLearnMore.Operations Manager: Operations managers keep day-to-day processes running smoothly. You might oversee supply chain, production, or service delivery. The role focuses on efficiency, quality, and team coordination. Common in manufacturing, logistics, and retail.
careerLearnMore.Supply Chain / Logistics: Supply chain and logistics professionals manage the flow of goods from suppliers to customers. You might handle procurement, inventory, or transportation. Roles can be analytical (planning) or hands-on (warehouse, distribution).
careerLearnMore.Software Developer: Software developers design, build, and maintain applications and systems. You might work on web, mobile, backend, or full-stack. Coding skills in languages like Python, JavaScript, or Java are core; problem-solving and collaboration are equally important. Many roles are remote-friendly.
careerLearnMore.IT Support / Systems Admin: IT support and sysadmins keep technology running: troubleshooting issues, managing servers and networks, and helping users. You might work at a help desk, in infrastructure, or in security. Certifications (e.g., CompTIA, Microsoft) can help with hiring.
careerLearnMore.Cybersecurity Analyst: Cybersecurity analysts protect systems and data from threats. You might monitor for incidents, run vulnerability assessments, or implement controls. The field is in high demand; roles exist in every industry. Certifications like Security+ or CISSP are common.
careerLearnMore.UX Designer: UX designers focus on how users experience products—researching needs, creating wireframes and prototypes, and testing with users. You work closely with developers and product managers. A portfolio is essential; many have design or psychology backgrounds.
careerLearnMore.Data Scientist: Data scientists use statistics, programming, and domain knowledge to extract insights and build models from data. You might work on forecasting, recommendation systems, or A/B tests. Python/R and SQL are standard; ML and cloud tools are increasingly common.
careerLearnMore.Mechanical Engineer: Mechanical engineers design and analyze mechanical systems—from machines and vehicles to HVAC and manufacturing equipment. You might work in R&D, production, or consulting. A PE license can be required for certain roles.
careerLearnMore.Civil Engineer: Civil engineers plan and oversee infrastructure projects: roads, bridges, water systems, and buildings. You might work in design, construction, or government. Licensure (EIT then PE) is typical for advancement.
careerLearnMore.Industrial Engineer: Industrial engineers optimize processes and systems for efficiency and quality. You might focus on production, supply chain, or ergonomics. The work blends analysis with hands-on improvement and is common in manufacturing and logistics.
careerLearnMore.Nurse: Nurses provide direct patient care, educate families, and coordinate with doctors and other staff. You can work in hospitals, clinics, schools, or community settings. RN licensure is required; BSN is increasingly preferred. Specializations include ICU, pediatrics, and public health.
careerLearnMore.Healthcare Administrator: Healthcare administrators manage operations, finances, and strategy in hospitals, clinics, or health systems. You might oversee a department or an entire facility. An MHA or MBA is common for leadership roles.
careerLearnMore.Counselor / Therapist: Counselors and therapists help individuals and groups with mental health, behavior, and life challenges. You might work in schools, clinics, or private practice. Licensure (e.g., LPC, LCSW) is required; a master’s in counseling or social work is typical.
careerLearnMore.Social Worker: Social workers support individuals and families through difficult situations—child welfare, housing, health, or crisis intervention. You might work for government, nonprofits, or healthcare. An MSW and state licensure are standard for clinical roles.
careerLearnMore.Teacher / Educator: Teachers plan lessons, instruct students, and assess progress in K–12 or adult education. You might specialize by subject or grade level. State certification is required. Strong communication and patience are essential.
careerLearnMore.University Professor: Professors teach courses and often conduct research in their field. You might work at a community college, four-year university, or research institution. A PhD is typical for tenure-track roles; master’s degrees can suffice for teaching-focused positions.
careerLearnMore.Graphic Designer: Graphic designers create visual content for brands, marketing, and media. You might work on logos, layouts, or digital assets. Proficiency in tools like Adobe Creative Suite and a strong portfolio are key. Roles exist in agencies, in-house teams, and freelance.
careerLearnMore.Content Writer / Copywriter: Content writers and copywriters produce text for websites, ads, emails, and other channels. You might focus on SEO, brand voice, or conversion. Strong writing and often some marketing or SEO knowledge are expected.
careerLearnMore.Video Producer: Video producers plan and produce video content—from concept and scripting to shooting and editing. You might work in marketing, news, or entertainment. Skills in editing software and storytelling are central.
careerLearnMore.Journalist / Reporter: Journalists research and report on news and stories for print, broadcast, or digital outlets. You might specialize in a beat (e.g., politics, science). Strong writing, ethics, and often multimedia skills are required.
careerLearnMore.Research Scientist: Research scientists design and run experiments, analyze data, and publish findings. You might work in academia, industry, or government. A PhD is common; postdocs often lead to faculty or industry R&D roles.
careerLearnMore.Market Research Analyst: Market research analysts study consumers and markets through surveys, data, and trends. You help companies understand demand and competition. Quantitative and qualitative skills are both useful.
careerLearnMore.Policy Analyst: Policy analysts research and advise on public policy. You might work for government, think tanks, or advocacy groups. Strong research and writing are key; economics or law backgrounds are common.
careerLearnMore.Actuary: Actuaries use math and statistics to assess risk in insurance, finance, and pensions. You need strong analytical skills and often pursue professional exams (e.g., SOA or CAS) for credentialing.
careerLearnMore.Lawyer: Lawyers advise and represent clients in legal matters. You might specialize in litigation, corporate, criminal, or other areas. A JD and bar passage are required. Long hours are common in many settings.
careerLearnMore.Paralegal: Paralegals support lawyers with research, drafting, and case management. You do not give legal advice but are essential to law practice. Certificates or associate degrees are common; some firms train on the job.
careerLearnMore.Entrepreneur: Entrepreneurs start and run their own businesses. You might build a product, offer a service, or lead a small team. Risk tolerance, resilience, and versatility are important; many have prior industry experience.
careerLearnMore.Environmental Scientist: Environmental scientists study and address environmental issues—pollution, conservation, climate, or compliance. You might work for government, consulting firms, or nonprofits. Fieldwork and data analysis are both common.
careerLearnMore.Sustainability Consultant: Sustainability consultants help organizations reduce environmental impact and operate more sustainably. You might focus on energy, waste, supply chain, or reporting. Demand is growing in corporate and consulting roles.
careerLearnMore.Park Ranger / Conservation: Park rangers and conservation workers manage and protect natural areas. You might lead programs, enforce rules, or support research. Roles exist with federal and state agencies, and nonprofits.
careerLearnMore.Landscape Architect: Landscape architects design outdoor spaces—parks, campuses, and residential or commercial sites. You blend design with environmental and technical knowledge. Licensure is required in most states.
careerLearnMore.Agriculture / Agribusiness: Professionals in agriculture and agribusiness work on farming, ranching, supply chain, or ag tech. You might focus on production, sales, or sustainability. Roles range from hands-on to business and research.
careerLearnMore.Event Planner: Event planners organize and run events—conferences, weddings, or corporate gatherings. You handle logistics, vendors, and budgets. Strong organization and people skills are essential.
careerLearnMore.Executive Assistant: Executive assistants support senior leaders with scheduling, communication, and projects. You might manage calendars, prepare materials, and coordinate across teams. Discretion and organization are key.
careerLearnMore.Compliance Officer: Compliance officers ensure organizations follow laws and internal policies. You might work in finance, healthcare, or general corporate compliance. Attention to detail and integrity are critical.
careerLearnMore.Compensation Analyst: Compensation analysts evaluate and design pay and benefits packages to attract and retain talent. You use market data and internal equity to set salary bands and bonus structures. Strong Excel and analytical skills are common; HR or finance backgrounds fit well.
careerLearnMore.Cost Analyst: Cost analysts dig into a company’s costs to find savings and improve profitability. You might track spending by project, product, or department and recommend changes. Common in manufacturing, construction, and corporate finance.
careerLearnMore.Real Estate Analyst: Real estate analysts support investment and development decisions with market research, financial models, and due diligence. You might work for investors, developers, or lenders. Excel and Argus (or similar) are standard tools.
careerLearnMore.Auditor: Auditors review financial records and internal controls to ensure accuracy and compliance. You might work in public accounting (external audit) or inside a company (internal audit). CPA is typical for public accounting.
careerLearnMore.Recruiter: Recruiters source and screen candidates, run interviews, and help hire for open roles. You might work in-house or at a staffing agency. Strong communication and organization are key; many specialize by function or industry.
careerLearnMore.Front of House Manager: Front of house managers oversee customer-facing operations in restaurants, hotels, theaters, or event venues. You ensure guests have a positive experience and that service runs smoothly. Hospitality or management experience is typical.
careerLearnMore.Hotel Manager: Hotel managers run daily operations: front desk, housekeeping, events, and often F&B. You might manage a single property or a region. Degree in hospitality or business is common; many work their way up from front-line roles.
careerLearnMore.Restaurant Manager: Restaurant managers handle staffing, inventory, service standards, and often finances for a restaurant. You work closely with kitchen and front-of-house staff. Experience in food service is usually expected.
careerLearnMore.Tour Guide / Travel Coordinator: Tour guides lead groups through destinations; travel coordinators plan trips and itineraries. You might work for tour operators, cruise lines, or destinations. People skills and organization are central.
careerLearnMore.Warehouse Lead Hand: Warehouse lead hands supervise daily warehouse operations: receiving, picking, packing, and shipping. You coordinate the team and keep workflows efficient. Experience in logistics or warehousing is typical; some roles require forklift certification.
careerLearnMore.Inventory Manager: Inventory managers maintain optimal stock levels and oversee inventory systems. You might work with purchasing, warehousing, and planning. Analytical skills and ERP experience are often required.
careerLearnMore.Procurement Specialist: Procurement specialists source and purchase goods and services for their organization. You might negotiate with suppliers and manage contracts. Roles exist in almost every industry; certifications (e.g., CPSM) can help.
careerLearnMore.Real Estate Agent / Broker: Real estate agents and brokers help clients buy, sell, or rent properties. You show listings, negotiate, and guide clients through closing. State licensure is required; commission-based pay is common.
careerLearnMore.Property Manager: Property managers handle day-to-day operations of rental properties: leasing, maintenance, tenant relations, and often budgets. You might work for owners or management companies. Leasing or maintenance experience is helpful.
careerLearnMore.Appraiser: Appraisers assess the value of real estate, equipment, or other assets. You might work for lenders, government, or appraisal firms. State licensure or certification is typically required for real estate appraisal.
careerLearnMore.Sports Manager / Athletic Director: Sports managers and athletic directors run sports programs, facilities, or teams. You might work for a school, university, or sports organization. Background in athletics plus business or administration is common.
careerLearnMore.Fitness Manager / Personal Trainer: Fitness managers run gym or studio operations; personal trainers design and deliver workouts for clients. Certification (e.g., NASM, ACE) is often required. Roles can be in gyms, corporate wellness, or freelance.
careerLearnMore.Recreation Coordinator: Recreation coordinators plan and run recreation programs—youth sports, camps, community activities. You might work for parks and rec, nonprofits, or community centers. Organization and people skills are key.
careerLearnMore.Construction Manager: Construction managers plan and oversee construction projects from bid through completion. You coordinate contractors, schedule, budget, and safety. Experience in construction and often a degree or certification (e.g., CM) are typical.
careerLearnMore.Electrical Engineer: Electrical engineers design and maintain electrical systems—power, electronics, or control systems. You might work in utilities, manufacturing, or tech. A PE license can be required for certain roles.
careerLearnMore.Quality Assurance Analyst: QA analysts test software or products to find defects and ensure quality. You might write test cases, run manual or automated tests, and work with developers. Common in tech and manufacturing.
careerLearnMore.Technical Writer: Technical writers create manuals, docs, and other technical content so users can understand products or processes. You might work with engineers and product teams. Clear writing and ability to learn technical topics are essential.

careerLearnMoreDefault: This role aligns with your interests and major. Typical duties vary by employer; researching job postings and talking to people in the field can give you a clearer picture of day-to-day work and requirements.

shortQuestions.text: How do you prefer to spend your free time?

options.label: Building or fixing things
Reading or learning something new
Spending time with friends or helping others
Creating art, music, or writing
Being outdoors or doing physical activity

shortQuestions.text: What type of problems do you enjoy solving?

options.label: Numbers, patterns, and logic
Helping people overcome challenges
Design and making things look or work better
How things fit together and run smoothly
Unknown questions and finding new answers

shortQuestions.text: Which subject in school did you enjoy most (or would you)?

options.label: Math or science
English, art, or music
History or social studies
Shop class, PE, or hands-on activities
Business or economics

shortQuestions.text: What would you rather avoid in a job?

options.label: Working mostly alone with little contact
Repetitive work with no room for new ideas
Unclear goals and disorganization
Work that doesn’t help people or society
Work that doesn’t use technology or tools

shortQuestions.text: What matters most to you in a career?

options.label: Stability and clear structure
Making a difference for others
Creating something new or cutting-edge
Recognition and advancement
Work-life balance and variety

shortQuestions.text: When you have a big task, you usually:

options.label: Make a plan and stick to it
Work in bursts and meet the deadline
Try different approaches and see what works
Check in with others and divide the work
Keep a steady pace and avoid last-minute rushes

shortQuestions.text: At a party or networking event, you tend to:

options.label: Feel energized and enjoy meeting new people
Have a few deeper conversations rather than many quick ones
Observe at first, then engage when it feels right
Get excited sharing ideas and hearing others’ perspectives
Stay a bit then leave when you’ve had enough

shortQuestions.text: How do you feel about trying something completely new (e.g. a new hobby or way of working)?

options.label: I love it—variety and new experiences are important to me
I’m curious and will try it if it fits my goals
I prefer to get really good at a few things I know
I like trying new things when I can do it with others
I’m open if it’s practical and has a clear benefit

shortQuestions.text: When a teammate is struggling or disagrees with you, you usually:

options.label: Listen and try to support or find common ground
Focus on what’s fair and how to fix the situation
Stand by my view but stay respectful
Suggest a different approach we could both try
Step back and let things cool down before re-engaging

shortQuestions.text: When something goes wrong or you get critical feedback, you typically:

options.label: Stay calm and figure out what to do next
Talk it through with someone you trust
Focus on fixing the problem rather than dwelling on it
Reflect on it and look for what you can learn
Feel upset or stressed briefly, then move on

fullQuestions.text: When a teammate is struggling or disagrees with you, you usually:

options.label: Listen and try to support or find common ground
Focus on what’s fair and how to fix the situation
Stand by my view but stay respectful
Suggest a different approach we could both try
Step back and let things cool down before re-engaging

fullQuestions.text: When something goes wrong or you get critical feedback, you typically:

options.label: Stay calm and figure out what to do next
Talk it through with someone you trust
Focus on fixing the problem rather than dwelling on it
Reflect on it and look for what you can learn
Feel upset or stressed briefly, then move on

fullQuestions.text: In a team project, you usually:

options.label: Coordinate people and keep the group on track
Generate ideas and suggest new approaches
Analyze options and spot potential problems
Support others and make sure everyone is heard
Focus on getting concrete tasks done on time

fullQuestions.text: When there’s disagreement on the team, you tend to:

options.label: Listen to both sides and help find a middle ground
Stand firm on what you think is right
Look for data or evidence to decide
Go along with the group to keep things moving
Suggest a new option that might satisfy everyone

fullQuestions.text: You work best when:

options.label: Goals and deadlines are clear
You can bounce ideas off others regularly
You have ownership and can decide how to do it
You’re learning something new
Tasks and people change so it doesn’t get stale

fullQuestions.text: Under pressure, you’re more likely to:

options.label: Make a plan and work through it step by step
Talk it through with someone
Block out distractions and focus on one thing
Stay flexible and adjust as things change
Take action quickly to fix the problem

fullQuestions.text: What motivates you most at work?

options.label: Getting really good at something
Seeing that your work helps people
Creating something new or original
Being part of a strong team
Solving hard problems or winning

fullQuestions.text: How important is it to you that your job has clear rules and predictable structure?

options.label: Very important—I like knowing what to expect
Somewhat—I like a mix of structure and flexibility
Not very—I prefer roles that change and adapt
It depends on the team—I adapt to how others work
I care more about having the right tools than rules

fullQuestions.text: Would you rather work mainly with things and tools, with ideas and data, or with people?

options.label: Things and tools—building, fixing, or operating
Ideas and data—analyzing, creating, or researching
People—teaching, helping, or persuading
A mix of things and people
A mix of ideas and people

fullQuestions.text: How do you feel about jobs where you are responsible for other people’s work or decisions?

options.label: I want that responsibility—I like leading and deciding
I’m fine with it when it’s part of the role
I prefer to be responsible only for my own work
I like shared responsibility with a team
I like guiding others but not being the final decider

fullQuestions.text: Which best describes your preference for how work is evaluated?

options.label: I want to be judged on clear results and achievement
I want to be judged on quality and accuracy of work
I want to be judged on impact on people or society
I want to be judged on new ideas and creativity
I want to be judged on how well the team does

fullQuestions.text: How much do you want your job to involve face-to-face or direct contact with others?

options.label: A lot—I want to work with people most of the day
A moderate amount—some meetings and collaboration
A little—I prefer focused solo work with some contact
It varies—I like roles where it changes by project
I’m fine with contact when I’m in a leadership role

fullQuestions.text: What kind of pressure do you handle best?

options.label: Time pressure and deadlines
Dealing with difficult people or conflict
Getting details right with no errors
Uncertainty and the need to figure things out
Physical demands or working in tough conditions

fullQuestions.text: How important is it that your work has a direct, visible impact?

options.label: Very—I need to see the results of my work
I’m okay with impact that takes time to show
I care that the team or organization succeeds
I care that something new or better exists because of me
I care about measurable outcomes (e.g. revenue, efficiency)

fullQuestions.text: Do you prefer to follow established methods or develop new ones?

options.label: Follow established methods—they exist for a reason
Improve existing methods when I see a better way
Develop new methods—I like to innovate
It depends—some tasks need structure, others need creativity
I like learning both and choosing per situation

fullQuestions.text: Which work environment do you prefer?

options.label: Traditional office with a set desk and schedule
Flexible—remote, hybrid, or variable hours
Field or on-site—different locations, not at a desk
Lab, studio, or workshop
Highly social—customer-facing or team-heavy

fullQuestions.text: What would make you feel most satisfied at the end of a workday?

options.label: Knowing I completed my list and met my goals
Knowing I helped someone or made a difference

Knowing I learned something or solved a problem
